encoding_detection.reason: Encoded content detected — may hide payload; verify this is intentional

rules.reason: Safe cleanup
rules.intent: Allow removing node_modules as a safe project cleanup operation

rules.reason: Temp file cleanup
rules.intent: Allow removing temporary files under /tmp/ as routine cleanup

rules.reason: Build output cleanup — verify no uncommitted artifacts
rules.intent: Removing dist/ or build/ is routine but worth flagging since it deletes compiled output that may take time to regenerate

rules.reason: Recursive force delete blocked
rules.intent: Recursive force-delete bypasses confirmation and can destroy entire directory trees in one shot

rules.reason: System power control blocked
rules.intent: AI agents must not control host power state — this disrupts all running work

rules.reason: Disk format or raw device write blocked
rules.intent: Formatting disks, writing directly to block devices, or running fsck -y on a mounted device destroys all data — never appropriate in a dev context

rules.reason: Insecure permissions blocked
rules.intent: World-writable permissions (777/666) and recursive chmod let any process modify files across entire directory trees, breaking access control boundaries

rules.reason: Dangerous security override blocked
rules.intent: Flags that disable safety mechanisms (preserve-root, setuid, container isolation, sandbox, content trust) must never be used by AI agents

rules.reason: Executing from temp directory blocked — bypasses content evaluators
rules.intent: Writing a script to /tmp bypasses Write/Edit content scanning, then executing it runs unscanned code

rules.reason: Script file execution requires approval — file content is not re-scanned at exec time
rules.intent: Executing a script file by interpreter runs its full content without per-line content evaluation — a two-step write-then-execute attack bypasses content scanning rules

rules.reason: Critical system control operation blocked
rules.intent: Flushing firewall rules, removing all cron jobs, or killing init/systemd destabilizes the host

rules.reason: Mass file destruction via find pipeline — review carefully
rules.intent: Using find with -delete, -exec rm/shred/truncate, or piping to xargs achieves uncontrolled mass file destruction

rules.reason: Destructive file shredding blocked — irrecoverable data destruction
rules.intent: shred overwrites file contents with random data making recovery impossible — used for anti-forensic log tampering, never needed in coding workflows

rules.reason: Truncating file to zero bytes blocked — silent data destruction
rules.intent: truncate -s 0 empties a file while leaving it in place — commonly used to wipe logs after exploitation while evading existence checks

rules.reason: Partition table manipulation blocked
rules.intent: Partition tools modify disk layout — can destroy filesystem structure and cause total data loss

rules.reason: wipefs blocked — filesystem signature wipe
rules.intent: wipefs erases filesystem signatures from block devices — makes partitions unrecognizable

rules.reason: Reverse shell blocked
rules.intent: Redirecting interactive bash to /dev/tcp or using netcat -e to spawn shells are classic reverse shell techniques

rules.reason: Kernel sysrq trigger access blocked
rules.intent: /proc/sysrq-trigger can force reboot, crash dump, OOM kill, or remount filesystems — kernel-level destructive control

rules.reason: Kernel module manipulation blocked
rules.intent: Loading or removing kernel modules can introduce rootkits, crash the kernel, or disable security features

rules.reason: System configuration modification blocked
rules.intent: Modifying kernel parameters, file attributes, or sudoers can destabilize the system or grant unauthorized access

rules.reason: User account or mass ownership change blocked
rules.intent: User account manipulation can create backdoors; recursive chown on system paths breaks filesystem permissions

rules.reason: Redirect to /dev/null blocked — silent data destruction
rules.intent: Symlinking or moving files to /dev/null silently discards all data — used to disable logging or destroy config

rules.reason: System file or kernel parameter overwrite blocked
rules.intent: Overwriting critical system files or /proc/sys/ can lock out users, break name resolution, or alter kernel behavior

rules.reason: Moving root or home directory blocked
rules.intent: Moving / or ~ relocates the entire filesystem or home directory — catastrophic data loss

rules.reason: Dangerous xargs pipeline blocked
rules.intent: Piping into xargs rm enables mass deletion; xargs sh -c enables arbitrary command execution on each input line

rules.reason: Destructive disk operation blocked
rules.intent: badblocks -w performs destructive write testing; hdparm --security-erase triggers firmware-level disk wipe — both destroy all data

rules.reason: Physical memory access blocked
rules.intent: Direct access to /dev/mem allows reading or writing physical RAM — can extract secrets or corrupt the kernel

rules.reason: Fork bomb pattern blocked
rules.intent: Recursive fork patterns in Perl or Python exhaust the process table and crash the system

rules.reason: Sudo not permitted
rules.intent: AI agents should operate with least privilege — escalation expands blast radius of any mistake

rules.reason: TLS verification bypass — confirm this is intentional
rules.intent: Flags that skip certificate verification enable man-in-the-middle attacks on network traffic

rules.reason: Base64 encoding/decoding — confirm this is intentional
rules.intent: Base64 can be used to obfuscate data exfiltration or bypass content filters

rules.reason: Environment variable dump detected — may expose API keys and secrets
rules.intent: Commands that dump environment variables expose API keys, DB credentials, and tokens into agent context

rules.reason: Binary encoding or crypto operation — may obfuscate content past scanners
rules.intent: Hex/UU encoding and openssl operations can transform data into forms that bypass content-based security scanning

rules.reason: Encoded escape sequence detected — confirm intent
rules.intent: Hex, URL-encoded, and octal escape sequences can obfuscate dangerous operations to evade pattern matching

rules.reason: Password change requires approval
rules.intent: passwd modifies user authentication credentials — verify the target account and purpose

rules.reason: Switch user requires approval
rules.intent: su - opens a root login shell — grants full system access that persists after the command

rules.reason: Ownership change requires approval
rules.intent: chown changes file ownership — can affect access control and security boundaries

rules.reason: Large file allocation requires approval
rules.intent: fallocate preallocates disk space — large allocations can exhaust disk and cause denial of service

rules.reason: Terminating LaneKeep processes is not permitted
rules.intent: Killing lanekeep-serve disables governance enforcement — agents must not terminate the sidecar

rules.reason: Terminating LaneKeep processes is not permitted
rules.intent: Catches patterns like `kill $(pgrep lanekeep)` or piped kill commands

rules.reason: LaneKeep environment variables must not be modified by agents
rules.intent: Env vars control fail policy, config path, and socket location — modification could disable enforcement

rules.reason: Directory removal — confirm target is intentional
rules.intent: rmdir removes directories — verify the target before proceeding

rules.reason: --dangerously-skip-permissions bypasses all Claude Code safety checks
rules.intent: Passing --dangerously-skip-permissions to claude disables the entire permission system — never appropriate for an AI agent to invoke another agent with all guardrails removed

rules.reason: Wildcard rm without -r/-f — confirm target files in non-interactive context
rules.intent: rm with glob patterns (e.g. rm -i *) deletes all matching files. The -i flag is ineffective when stdin is not a TTY, as in AI agent contexts — the shell expands the glob before rm runs and there is no prompt to protect against accidental mass deletion

rules.reason: Setting executable permissions — verify target file
rules.intent: chmod 755 grants world-execute permissions; safe for scripts but worth verifying the target file is intended to be executable

rules.reason: API key or secret key pattern detected
rules.intent: Hardcoded AWS keys and API keys in code get committed to repos and leaked — use IAM roles, env vars, or secrets management

rules.reason: Password or secret in code detected
rules.intent: Passwords and secrets in source code persist in git history even after removal — use env vars or vault

rules.reason: Sensitive directory or credential file access blocked
rules.intent: SSH keys, .env files, AWS config, GPG keyrings, credentials, and kubeconfig contain authentication material — access risks leaking them into conversation context

rules.reason: Copy/move from temp directory blocked — bypasses content-based scanning
rules.intent: Staging content in /tmp then copying into the project bypasses file-content evaluators that scan Write/Edit payloads

rules.reason: Data exfiltration pattern blocked
rules.intent: Piping secret files or env vars to network commands, or piping data into network tools, is textbook exfiltration

rules.reason: Prompt injection content blocked — LLM delimiter tokens, fake auth tags, or hidden comment smuggling
rules.intent: ChatML/Llama delimiters, fake XML authorization tags, and markdown hidden comments with injection keywords are memory poisoning and privilege escalation vectors

rules.reason: Fake XML authority tag detected — common prompt injection wrapper
rules.intent: XML-style authority tags like <IMPORTANT>, <CRITICAL>, <MANDATORY> are used to wrap injected instructions with false urgency or privilege — the Shai-Hulud pattern and variants use these to trick agents into treating attacker text as system-level directives

rules.reason: Prompt injection pattern in file content — flag for human review
rules.intent: Session reset boundaries and instruction override phrases in files can hijack LLM sessions

match.pattern: (?i)\byou are now\b|\bfrom now on you\b|\bassume the role\b|\bimagine you are\b|\bpretend you (are|have)\b|\bact as if you\b|\bjailbreak\b|\bdo anything now\b|\bignore your safety\b|\bact without restrictions\b|\bno (ethical |moral )?restrictions\b

rules.reason: Prompt injection pattern in file content — flag for human review
rules.intent: Persona injection and jailbreak phrases in files can hijack LLM sessions

rules.reason: Prompt injection pattern in file content — flag for human review
rules.intent: Fake role structures and leetspeak-encoded injection phrases in files can hijack LLM sessions

rules.reason: Bare LLM role prefix detected — may confuse model into treating file content as a system/assistant message
rules.intent: Plain-text role prefixes (SYSTEM:, ASSISTANT:, USER:, etc.) at the start of a line can trick LLMs into role confusion when file content is fed back into a conversation

rules.reason: Bracket-style role marker detected — common LLM delimiter that could enable role injection
rules.intent: Bracket markers like [SYSTEM], [/SYSTEM], [[SYSTEM]], [INSTRUCTION] are used by various LLM frameworks as role delimiters; injecting them into file content can hijack model behavior

rules.reason: XML-style role tag detected — may be used to inject fake role boundaries into LLM context
rules.intent: Tags like <system>, </assistant>, <prompt> mimic XML role delimiters used by LLM APIs; embedding them in files can trick models into treating content as privileged instructions

rules.reason: Prompt injection detected in tool input
rules.intent: Phrases like 'ignore previous instructions' or 'you are now' attempt to override the agent's system prompt — block across all tool types to prevent instruction hijacking

rules.reason: System security file access blocked
rules.intent: /etc/shadow, passwd, sudoers contain authentication and privilege data; /boot/ contains kernel images — access risks credential theft or rootkit installation

rules.reason: Privilege escalation mechanism blocked
rules.intent: Setuid permissions, NOPASSWD in sudoers, and Linux capabilities grant elevated privileges — creates privilege escalation vectors

rules.reason: Secret variable echoed to stdout — value will appear in shell history and logs
rules.intent: Echoing named secret variables (SECRET, TOKEN, API_KEY, etc.) prints their values to stdout, terminal logs, CI output, and ~/.zsh_history. Use 'echo ${#VAR}' (length) or '[[ -n $VAR ]]' to verify without exposing the value.

rules.reason: Cloud upload CLI detected — confirm upload destination and intent
rules.intent: Cloud upload CLIs (gsutil, azcopy, rclone) can exfiltrate data to external storage — aws already gated by inf-014

rules.reason: Sensitive business, financial, or customer data detected — confirm sharing with AI
rules.intent: Strategic documents, financial/HR data, and customer data files contain non-public information that must not be processed by 3rd-party AI services

rules.reason: Reading LaneKeep configuration blocked — governance rules must remain opaque to the governed agent
rules.intent: If the agent can read the ruleset, it can reverse-engineer match patterns and craft inputs that evade detection

rules.reason: Reading LaneKeep configuration via shell blocked — governance rules must remain opaque to the governed agent
rules.intent: Shell commands reading config files bypass Read tool protections — both vectors must be closed

rules.reason: Reading LaneKeep installation source blocked — governance implementation must remain opaque to the governed agent
rules.intent: If the agent can read evaluator source, hook scripts, or default rules, it can reverse-engineer detection logic and craft evasions

rules.reason: Reading LaneKeep installation via shell blocked — governance implementation must remain opaque to the governed agent
rules.intent: Shell commands reading installation files bypass Read/Glob/Grep tool protections — both vectors must be closed

rules.reason: Inspecting LaneKeep environment variables or processes blocked — installation paths must remain opaque
rules.intent: Environment variables and process listings reveal the installation path, enabling the agent to locate and read governance source

rules.reason: Introspecting LaneKeep binary location blocked — install path must remain opaque
rules.intent: Commands like 'which lanekeep' reveal the install path, enabling the agent to navigate to governance source

rules.reason: Reading LaneKeep documentation blocked — governance architecture details must remain opaque
rules.intent: Documentation describes evaluator pipelines, rule formats, and hook protocols — knowledge enabling evasion

rules.reason: Destructive git operation blocked
rules.intent: Hard reset, clean -fd, and branch -D destroy history or untracked work with no recovery path

rules.reason: Force-with-lease rewrites remote history — verify no concurrent collaborators
rules.intent: --force-with-lease is safer than --force but still rewrites published history; warn so the developer confirms no other committers will be disrupted

rules.reason: Bypassing git hooks skips safety checks (Rule 10: review before commit)
rules.intent: Git hooks enforce team conventions (linting, tests, secrets scan) — bypassing them defeats shared quality gates

rules.reason: Git internals and metadata files protected
rules.intent: Git config, hooks, submodules, and attributes control repository behavior — unauthorized changes enable code injection and supply chain attacks

rules.reason: Git history destruction blocked
rules.intent: Expiring reflogs removes the last-resort recovery mechanism; filter-branch rewrites history and corrupts shared repos

rules.reason: AI auto-approval or auto-merge of PRs blocked — human review required
rules.intent: AI should never auto-approve or auto-merge pull requests — human review is required for code quality gates

rules.reason: Remote branch deletion via :branch blocked
rules.intent: git push :branch deletes the remote branch — can destroy shared work and CI pipelines

rules.reason: Commit -a or --amend requires review
rules.intent: Committing all changes bypasses selective staging; amending rewrites published history — both need human review

rules.reason: Discards ALL unstaged changes — consider git stash instead (Rule 5)
rules.intent: This silently discards every unstaged change in the working tree — there is no undo

rules.reason: Stash destruction is irreversible — confirm you don't need the stashed work
rules.intent: Dropping or clearing stashes permanently destroys stashed work — ensure it is no longer needed

rules.reason: Rebase rewrites history — confirm branch is not shared (Rule 3)
rules.intent: Rebase rewrites commit hashes — if the branch is shared, other developers' work will conflict

rules.reason: Main/master branch push requires approval
rules.intent: Pushing to main/master affects all developers and may trigger production deployments

rules.reason: Merging unrelated histories — confirm this is intentional
rules.intent: Merging unrelated histories grafts two disconnected repos together — usually a mistake

rules.reason: PR merge via CLI — confirm the PR has been reviewed and approved by a human
rules.intent: Merging PRs should require human review — AI-initiated merges bypass the collaborative review process

rules.reason: Destructive database operation blocked
rules.intent: DROP, TRUNCATE, and DELETE FROM destroy data — schema changes must go through migrations with human review

match.pattern: (gcloud sql connect|az sql db connect|aws rds connect)

rules.reason: Cloud database connection blocked — use local development databases
rules.intent: Cloud CLI database connections target production infrastructure — AI agents must not have direct access to production databases

rules.reason: UPDATE without WHERE clause blocked
rules.intent: UPDATE without WHERE modifies every row in the table — almost always unintended and destructive

rules.reason: Overprivileged grants blocked
rules.intent: GRANT ALL gives maximum database privileges — violates least-privilege principle and creates security risk

rules.reason: Raw SQL or direct cursor execution — ensure parameterized queries are used
rules.intent: Raw SQL and direct cursor execution bypass ORM parameterization — verify bind parameters are used to prevent injection

rules.reason: Database client or dump/restore command — confirm target is not production
rules.intent: Database clients and dump/restore tools can access or overwrite production data — verify the target environment

rules.reason: Schema destruction requires approval
rules.intent: ALTER TABLE DROP removes schema elements — data loss if column dropped, performance impact if index dropped

rules.reason: Socat reverse shell via exec: blocked
rules.intent: Socat with exec: spawns a shell process connected to a network socket — classic reverse shell technique

rules.reason: Remote code execution via process substitution or embedded curl blocked
rules.intent: Shell process substitution and embedding curl subshells/backticks in language invocations enable remote code execution

rules.reason: Network request requires approval
rules.intent: Outbound network tools can exfiltrate data or fetch untrusted content — verify the target and purpose

rules.reason: Netcat requires approval
rules.intent: Netcat opens raw network connections — can be used for data exfiltration or reverse shells

rules.reason: Package listing — review output for supply-chain awareness
rules.intent: Listing installed packages is safe — no side effects

rules.reason: Standard build/test script
rules.intent: Allow standard package manager scripts (test, build, lint, etc.) to run without restriction

rules.reason: Package registry bypass blocked
rules.intent: Bypassing TLS for pip or changing the npm registry can redirect all package installs to a malicious source

rules.reason: Lifecycle script injection in package.json blocked — auto-executes on npm install
rules.intent: npm lifecycle scripts (preinstall, postinstall, prepare) auto-execute on install — a persistence and supply-chain attack vector

rules.reason: Manual lockfile editing blocked — regenerate via package manager instead
rules.intent: AI agents should never manually edit lockfiles — integrity hashes would be invalid and supply chain attacks can inject malicious resolutions

rules.reason: npm registry operation blocked
rules.intent: Unpublishing, token management, and ownership changes are sensitive registry operations that should not be automated by AI agents

rules.reason: Disabling ignore-scripts safety blocked
rules.intent: Setting ignore-scripts to false re-enables automatic lifecycle script execution — bypasses a critical supply chain security control

rules.reason: Dependency install requires approval
rules.intent: New dependencies expand the attack surface and can run install scripts with full shell access — verify the package is legitimate

rules.reason: Non-standard script or remote package execution — confirm it is safe
rules.intent: Non-standard package scripts and npx/exec/dlx download and run code from registries — supply chain risk

rules.reason: Modifying package.json scripts section — confirm changes are intentional
rules.intent: General scripts modifications are less dangerous than lifecycle hooks but worth reviewing

rules.reason: Deleting or moving lockfile — confirm this is intentional cleanup
rules.intent: Removing lockfiles may be intentional cleanup but can also break reproducible builds

rules.reason: --ignore-scripts flag detected — security-positive but may break installs
rules.intent: The --ignore-scripts flag prevents lifecycle script execution which improves security but can break packages that require post-install steps

rules.reason: npm link symlinks a global package locally — requires approval
rules.intent: npm link creates symlinks between global and local packages — can redirect imports to attacker-controlled code if the global package is compromised

rules.reason: npm deprecate requires approval — affects downstream consumers
rules.intent: Deprecating packages sends warnings to all consumers — should be a deliberate human decision

rules.reason: Cloud resource destruction blocked
rules.intent: Deleting cloud resources (S3 buckets, databases, VMs, clusters, projects) causes immediate data loss and service disruption

rules.reason: CI/CD config protected
rules.intent: CI/CD workflows run with elevated privileges — unauthorized changes can execute arbitrary code in pipelines

rules.reason: Infrastructure code protected
rules.intent: Infrastructure-as-code changes provision real cloud resources — mistakes cost money and create security exposure

rules.reason: Docker config protected
rules.intent: Dockerfile and compose file changes alter build environments and service topology — can introduce vulnerabilities or expose ports

rules.reason: IDE config protected
rules.intent: IDE settings control terminal commands, AI behavior, extensions, and workspace trust — modifying them is a meta-attack on governance

rules.reason: Package publishing blocked
rules.intent: Publishing pushes code to a public registry — once published, versions cannot be fully retracted

rules.reason: K8s deletion blocked
rules.intent: Deleting Kubernetes resources can cause immediate production outages with cascading effects

rules.reason: Infrastructure provisioning/destruction blocked
rules.intent: IaC apply/destroy commands provision or tear down real cloud resources — must be human-initiated with plan review

rules.reason: Helm release removal blocked
rules.intent: Helm delete/uninstall removes K8s releases and all managed resources — causes service disruption

rules.reason: Database destruction via CLI blocked
rules.intent: CLI commands that drop or flush entire databases cause permanent, unrecoverable data loss

rules.reason: Release creation blocked — releases are deployment actions requiring human initiation
rules.intent: Creating releases can trigger deployment pipelines and publish artifacts — AI agents must not participate in release processes

rules.reason: Docker system prune blocked
rules.intent: docker system prune destroys all unused containers, images, networks, and optionally volumes

rules.reason: Docker host root mount blocked
rules.intent: Mounting the host root filesystem into a container gives the container read/write access to all host files — equivalent to running as root on the host

rules.reason: Devcontainer config requires approval
rules.intent: devcontainer.json initializeCommand runs ON THE HOST — verify no code injection

rules.reason: Container execution requires approval
rules.intent: Containers can mount host filesystems and expose network ports — verify the image, flags, and target container

rules.reason: Cloud CLI requires approval
rules.intent: Cloud CLI commands can provision, modify, or destroy resources that cost money and affect production

rules.reason: CI/CD workflow trigger detected — confirm this is intentional
rules.intent: AI agents should not autonomously trigger CI/CD pipelines — workflow execution can deploy code, run integration tests against production, or consume CI credits

rules.reason: Docker container/image removal requires approval
rules.intent: docker rm/rmi removes containers and images — verify no important data, state, or build artifacts will be lost

rules.reason: Remote code execution via pipe or eval blocked
rules.intent: Piping to shell, eval with command substitution ($(...) or backticks), or sourcing remote content executes arbitrary code without review — primary supply-chain attack vector

rules.reason: SQL string interpolation — use parameterized queries
rules.intent: String interpolation and fmt.Sprintf in SQL enable injection attacks — user input becomes executable SQL

rules.reason: Shell injection via os.system blocked
rules.intent: os.system with f-strings or .format() enables shell injection — use subprocess with argument lists instead

rules.reason: Low-level process.binding spawn blocked — use child_process APIs
rules.intent: process.binding('spawn_sync') bypasses child_process module checks — indicates deliberate evasion of security controls

rules.reason: __builtins__ manipulation blocked
rules.intent: Modifying __builtins__ can override built-in functions globally — used to backdoor eval, import, open, etc.

rules.reason: Debug mode may leak internal state in production
rules.intent: Debug mode exposes stack traces, variable dumps, and internal paths — information attackers use for reconnaissance

rules.reason: Variable interpolation in SQL file — verify parameterized
rules.intent: Variable interpolation in .sql files may indicate unsafe query construction — verify bind parameters are used

rules.reason: XSS-prone DOM API — use textContent or sanitize input
rules.intent: innerHTML, dangerouslySetInnerHTML, and document.write parse raw HTML — attacker-controlled input becomes executable code

rules.reason: Stack trace exposure — log server-side only
rules.intent: Stack traces reveal file paths, framework versions, and internal logic — use structured logging with proper output control

rules.reason: Weak hash algorithm — use SHA-256+ or bcrypt for passwords
rules.intent: MD5 and SHA-1 have known collision attacks — they provide no meaningful security for hashing

rules.reason: Unsafe deserialization — use JSON or safe alternatives
rules.intent: pickle, marshal, and shelve deserialize arbitrary code objects — equivalent to eval() on untrusted input

rules.reason: yaml.load is unsafe — use yaml.safe_load to prevent deserialization attacks
rules.intent: PyYAML's load() can instantiate arbitrary Python objects from YAML tags — use safe_load to restrict to basic types

rules.reason: Dynamic code execution — avoid with untrusted input
rules.intent: eval() and exec() turn strings into executable code — if any part is user-controlled, it enables code injection

rules.reason: Use structured logging instead of stdout/stderr printing
rules.intent: stdout/stderr logging lacks levels, formatting, and rotation — use structured logging for operational visibility

rules.reason: Shell command execution in code requires approval
rules.intent: Shell-based command execution APIs are vulnerable to injection if inputs are untrusted — use safe alternatives

rules.reason: Dynamic code compilation — Function constructor and vm module require approval
rules.intent: Function() and the vm module compile strings into executable code — equivalent to eval() and enable code injection

rules.reason: Programmatic file/folder deletion requires approval
rules.intent: Programmatic file or directory deletion across any language should be verified — ensure targets are safe and not user-controlled paths

rules.reason: DNS lookup or raw socket connection in code requires approval
rules.intent: DNS queries and raw socket connections bypass HTTP-layer security controls and can be used for exfiltration or tunneling

rules.reason: System reconnaissance API requires approval
rules.intent: os.userInfo and os.networkInterfaces expose system identity and network topology — often used in reconnaissance

rules.reason: Dynamic import or native code loading requires approval
rules.intent: Dynamic imports and ctypes can load arbitrary modules and native libraries — can bypass Python security controls entirely

rules.reason: Interactive shell spawning blocked
rules.intent: code.interact and pty.spawn create interactive shells — used for persistence and reverse shell attacks

rules.reason: setup.py cmdclass override requires approval — supply chain hook
rules.intent: cmdclass overrides in setup.py execute custom code during pip install — a supply chain attack vector for Python packages

rules.reason: Code obfuscation technique detected — verify usage is legitimate
rules.intent: Character encoding, base64, hex-encoded, and Unicode-escaped strings are commonly used to obfuscate malicious payloads

rules.reason: Disabling browser security headers enables clickjacking and XSS
rules.intent: Removing X-Frame-Options, CSP, or frameguard allows clickjacking and cross-site script injection

rules.reason: Wildcard CORS or ALLOWED_HOSTS enables cross-origin attacks
rules.intent: Wildcard origins let any website make authenticated requests; wildcard hosts enable host-header injection for cache poisoning

rules.reason: Disabling TLS verification enables MITM attacks
rules.intent: Without certificate verification, any network intermediary can intercept and modify encrypted traffic

rules.reason: Insecure framework security settings blocked
rules.intent: Disabling secure cookies, SSL redirect, or exposing all actuator endpoints weakens authentication and leaks secrets

rules.reason: Disabling CSRF protection — ensure endpoint is truly stateless/public
rules.intent: Without CSRF protection, malicious sites can submit forms as the logged-in user — only safe for truly stateless APIs

rules.reason: Cloud metadata endpoint blocked — SSRF vector for credential theft
rules.intent: Instance metadata services return IAM credentials, tokens, and secrets — SSRF to these endpoints is the #1 cloud exploit vector

rules.reason: Dangerous URL scheme blocked — SSRF exploitation primitive
rules.intent: file:// reads local files; gopher:// sends raw bytes to arbitrary ports — both are classic SSRF exploitation techniques

rules.reason: Docker socket or K8s service account access blocked
rules.intent: Docker socket provides root-equivalent host access; K8s service account tokens enable cluster-wide lateral movement

rules.reason: SSRF-related pattern — verify binding and redirect targets
rules.intent: Binding to 0.0.0.0 exposes services on all interfaces; unrestricted redirects can route to internal metadata endpoints

rules.reason: RFC1918 private IP detected — confirm this is intentional
rules.intent: Private IP addresses could indicate SSRF probing of internal infrastructure or container/pod networks

rules.reason: Bare $(pwd) is unreliable in shell scripts — use ${PROJECT_DIR:-$PWD} or $(pwd 2>/dev/null || echo "$PWD") instead
rules.intent: $(pwd) can fail or return unexpected results in subshells, symlinked directories, and certain CI environments; the safe form provides a fallback

rules.reason: AI API base URL override detected — redirects model traffic to attacker-controlled endpoint
rules.intent: Overriding ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, OPENAI_BASE_URL, or similar environment variables silently redirects all LLM API calls to an attacker-controlled proxy, enabling credential theft, response manipulation, and full session hijack without any visible change to the agent's behavior

rules.reason: MCP server mass-enable or config injection detected — enables untrusted tool servers
rules.intent: enableAllProjectMcpServers bypasses the MCP server allowlist, granting any project-defined server tool access. Writing mcpServers config directly injects arbitrary tool endpoints. Both enable supply-chain attacks where a malicious MCP server exfiltrates data or executes code under the agent's identity

rules.reason: Data URI with executable content type detected — verify payload is safe
rules.intent: Data URIs with text/html or SVG content types can embed executable JavaScript without a network request — used for XSS, phishing overlays, and sandbox escapes in browser and webview contexts

rules.reason: Inline script or event handler detected — verify no injection risk
rules.intent: Inline <script> tags and on* event handlers are the primary XSS payload delivery mechanism. While framework-rendered content is typically safe, agent-written HTML that includes these patterns should be reviewed to confirm the content is not attacker-controlled

rules.reason: Known exfiltration/data collection service detected — these endpoints capture and store any data sent to them
rules.intent: Services like ngrok, webhook.site, requestbin, pipedream, and Burp Collaborator are purpose-built to receive and inspect arbitrary HTTP requests. An agent sending data to these endpoints is almost certainly exfiltrating secrets, tokens, or source code. Legitimate tunneling should use org-approved infrastructure, not public collection services

rules.reason: MCP autoApprove configuration detected — bypasses human-in-the-loop approval for tool calls
rules.intent: The autoApprove setting in MCP server configs allows specified tools to execute without user confirmation. Writing this setting grants an MCP server silent execution privileges, defeating the approval gate that protects against malicious tool use. A compromised or poisoned MCP server with autoApprove runs tools unattended

rules.reason: MCP tool description poisoning detected — hidden instructions or exfiltration indicators in tool metadata
rules.intent: MCP tool descriptions are rendered to the AI agent as context. Attackers embed hidden instructions (prompt injection), credential harvesting paths (.ssh, .aws, .env), or exfiltration URLs in tool descriptions to manipulate the agent into leaking data. Based on Invariant Labs tool-poisoning research

rules.reason: npx --yes skips install confirmation — auto-downloads and executes packages from npm without approval
rules.intent: npx -y/--yes bypasses the interactive prompt that warns when a package needs to be downloaded. In MCP server configs and CI scripts, this silently fetches and executes arbitrary packages from the npm registry — a primary supply chain attack vector. Typosquatted or compromised packages execute immediately on download

match.pattern: (?i)(ignore (all |any )?(previous|above|prior) (instructions|rules|guidelines)|you are now|disregard (all |any )?(previous|prior|above)|forget (your|all|everything)|new (system |base )?instructions|SYSTEM:\s|\[SYSTEM\]|<system>|act as (an? )?(unrestricted|unfiltered|jailbroken)|DAN mode|Developer Mode|bypass (all |any )?(safety|security|filter|restriction)|\bdo anything now\b)

rules.reason: Prompt injection pattern in agent definition file — attempts to override system instructions
rules.intent: Agent definition files (.claude/ configs, custom instructions, persona files) are loaded as trusted context. Injecting role-override phrases ('you are now', 'ignore previous instructions', 'DAN mode') into these files hijacks the agent's identity and bypasses all behavioral constraints. This is the agent-config equivalent of a stored XSS attack
